items.title: Implementation Documents
items.summary: Implementation Documents Browse the AI Act online using our AI Act Explorer This page lists all documents associated with the implementation of the AI Act. It will be updated as new documents are published. Many of the documents on this page are produced in response to ‘tasks’ set out for the AI ...
items.relevanceReason: This is a resource page for AI Act implementation documents, which will contain concrete obligations on AI software providers, conformity assessment procedures, technical documentation requirements, and enforcement guidance. A developer building AI systems needs to monitor these documents as they specify regulatory requirements their systems must meet.

items.title: EDPS Guidance for Risk Management of Artificial Intelligence Systems
items.summary: Structured AI risk management guidance covering accountability, procurement, deployment, and risk controls. One of the best official European documents on AI risk management.
items.relevanceReason: Comprehensive AI risk management framework — accountability, procurement, and deployment controls

items.title: EBA report on AI Act implications for EU banking and payments sector
items.summary: Maps the AI Act against banking-sector obligations and supervisory structures. Covers how banking-specific requirements interact with horizontal AI Act obligations.
items.relevanceReason: Maps AI Act obligations onto banking software — essential for developers serving financial institutions

items.title: EDPS TechDispatch: Human Oversight of Automated Decision-Making
items.summary: Highlights the need for standardised frameworks and metrics to assess whether human oversight of automated systems is actually effective.
items.relevanceReason: Defines measurable criteria for human oversight effectiveness — affects how developers design oversight interfaces

items.title: EIOPA Opinion on AI Governance and Risk Management in insurance
items.summary: Supervisory expectations for AI governance and risk management in the insurance sector. Applies a risk-based, proportionate approach.
items.relevanceReason: AI governance obligations specific to insurance — risk-based approach with sector-specific controls

items.title: Guidelines on scope of obligations for providers of general-purpose AI models
items.summary: Commission guidelines on GPAI provider obligations: technical documentation, copyright compliance, training data summaries. For systemic risk models: risk assessment, incident reporting, cybersecurity.
items.relevanceReason: Defines obligations for anyone building or substantially modifying general-purpose AI models

items.title: EDPB: AI Privacy Risks & Mitigations — Large Language Models
items.summary: Practical risk treatment for LLM deployments. Discusses deployer responsibilities in relation to provider risk treatment, fine-tuning, and retraining.
items.relevanceReason: Specific LLM privacy risks and mitigations — directly actionable for developers deploying language models

items.title: Commission Guidelines on the definition of an AI system (C/2025/3076)
items.summary: Commission guidelines for determining whether a software system constitutes an AI system under the AI Act. Essential for scope analysis — answers the threshold question of whether the Act applies.
items.relevanceReason: Determines whether a software system falls under AI Act obligations — the first question every developer must answer

items.title: Commission Guidelines on prohibited AI practices (C/2025/861)
items.summary: Commission guidelines clarifying which AI practices are prohibited under the AI Act, including social scoring, manipulative AI, emotion recognition in workplaces and education, and untargeted scraping for facial recognition databases.
items.relevanceReason: Defines what AI software must not do — developers must check systems against these prohibitions

items.title: AI Literacy obligations under Article 4 of the AI Act
items.summary: Article 4 requires providers and deployers to ensure sufficient AI literacy of staff dealing with AI systems. Already in force since February 2025, with enforcement from August 2026.
items.relevanceReason: Active compliance obligation — providers and deployers must ensure staff AI literacy

items.title: Switzerland decides on sector-specific AI regulation and Council of Europe Convention ratification
items.summary: Switzerland decided to ratify the Council of Europe AI Convention and continue with a sector-specific approach rather than copying the EU AI Act. OFCOM tasked with coordinating the Swiss framework.
items.relevanceReason: Defines Switzerland's AI regulatory direction — sector-specific approach with international treaty obligations

items.title: FINMA Guidance 08/2024: Governance and risk management when using AI
items.summary: FINMA supervisory guidance on AI governance in financial institutions. Explicitly addresses defined thresholds, validation methods, correctness and ongoing quality of outputs, and monitoring changes in input data.
items.relevanceReason: Defines concrete AI governance obligations: thresholds, validation, output quality monitoring, and input drift detection

items.title: EDPB Opinion 28/2024 on AI models and personal data
items.summary: Addresses when AI models may be considered anonymous, whether legitimate interests can support model training, and consequences of unlawful personal data processing in model development.
items.relevanceReason: Directly governs how developers may train AI models with personal data — core reading for any ML team

items.title: Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 — the EU AI Act enters into force
items.summary: The EU AI Act entered into force, establishing the world's first comprehensive legal framework for AI. Sets risk-based obligations for providers and deployers including conformity assessment, risk management, documentation, logging, human oversight, and declared accuracy metrics.
items.relevanceReason: The binding legal framework defining all provider and deployer obligations for AI software in the EU

items.title: FDPIC confirms current data protection law directly applicable to AI
items.summary: The Swiss Federal Data Protection Commissioner states that current Swiss data protection law is directly applicable to AI-supported processing, covering monitoring, data quality, lawful training and use, and ongoing controls.
items.relevanceReason: Swiss data protection law already applies to AI — no need to wait for AI-specific legislation

items.title: ESMA Statement on AI in investment services (May 2024)
items.summary: Initial guidance for firms using AI in investment services for retail clients. Clearest sectoral supervisory document for securities and investment markets.
items.relevanceReason: Specific supervisory expectations for AI in investment services — affects software serving financial clients

items.title: EDPB Report of the ChatGPT Taskforce
items.summary: Shows how EU data protection authorities think operationally about generative AI services. Covers governance, transparency, lawful basis, accuracy, and safeguards for LLM deployments.
items.relevanceReason: Practical supervisory expectations for deploying generative AI — transparency, accuracy, and safeguards

items.title: ISO/IEC 42001:2023 — AI Management System standard published
items.summary: International standard for AI management systems covering risk management, documentation, and continuous improvement for organisations developing or using AI systems.
items.relevanceReason: Certification standard for AI management systems — directly applicable to AI software vendors

items.title: NIST AI Risk Management Framework (AI RMF 1.0)
items.summary: Framework for managing AI risks emphasising Govern, Map, Measure, and Manage functions. Unusually strong on measurement and ongoing risk monitoring, including thresholding, validation, and drift detection.
items.relevanceReason: Best-practice engineering framework for AI risk measurement, monitoring, drift detection, and validation
